quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Balanced equation"?

options.text: Acid: produces H⁺(aq); base: produces OH⁻(aq); limited to aqueous solutions
The experimental measurement of heat changes using an insulated container; q = mcΔT for solution calorimetry
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Balanced equation" is defined as: A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Stoichiometric coefficient"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
ΔTb = iKbm; solute particles raise the boiling point by lowering the vapor pressure; i = van't Hoff factor
ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed); positive ΔH if more energy is needed to break bonds than is released forming new ones
The numbers placed before formulas in a balanced equation indicating the mole ratio of reactants and products

quiz_questions.explanation: "Stoichiometric coefficient" is defined as: The numbers placed before formulas in a balanced equation indicating the mole ratio of reactants and products. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Half-reaction method"?

options.text: Balancing redox equations by separating into oxidation and reduction half-reactions and balancing electrons transferred
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)
FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges

quiz_questions.explanation: "Half-reaction method" is defined as: Balancing redox equations by separating into oxidation and reduction half-reactions and balancing electrons transferred. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Net ionic equation"?

options.text: An equation showing only the species that change during a reaction; spectator ions are omitted
A substance that increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower Ea; regenerated and not consumed
The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients; temperature-dependent
Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases

quiz_questions.explanation: "Net ionic equation" is defined as: An equation showing only the species that change during a reaction; spectator ions are omitted. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Spectator ion"?

options.text: An ion present in solution that does not participate in the reaction; appears unchanged on both sides of the complete ionic equation
A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality
An advanced model of metallic bonding: overlapping atomic orbitals form continuous energy bands; partial filling of bands allows electron mobility
A measure of molecular polarity; vector sum of all bond dipoles; symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄) can have polar bonds but no net dipole

quiz_questions.explanation: "Spectator ion" is defined as: An ion present in solution that does not participate in the reaction; appears unchanged on both sides of the complete ionic equation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass"?

options.text: End point
Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)
Balanced equation
Catalyst effect

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Balanced equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The numbers placed before formulas in a balanced equation indicating the mole ratio of reactants and products"?

options.text: pKa
Viscosity
Stoichiometric coefficient
Mole

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Stoichiometric coefficient".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Balancing redox equations by separating into oxidation and reduction half-reactions and balancing electrons transferred"?

options.text: Half-reaction method
Phase diagram
Limiting reagent
Triple point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Half-reaction method".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An equation showing only the species that change during a reaction; spectator ions are omitted"?

options.text: Gibbs free energy (ΔG)
Net ionic equation
Equilibrium vs. completion
Mole

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Net ionic equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An ion present in solution that does not participate in the reaction; appears unchanged on both sides of the complete ionic equation"?

options.text: Collision theory
Spectator ion
Stoichiometric conversion
Catalyst effect

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Spectator ion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Balancing Chemical Equations is accurate?

options.text: Molecular Geometry is predicted by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
Oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions involve electron transfer; oxidation is loss of electrons (OIL), reduction is gain (RIG).
pH and pOH provide logarithmic scales for expressing H⁺ and OH⁻ concentrations. pH = -log[H⁺], pOH = -log[OH⁻], and pH + pOH = 14.00 at 25°C.
Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Balancing Chemical Equations as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Equations are balanced by adjusting stoichiometric coefficients (never subscripts).
Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and geometry.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Balancing Chemical Equations as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.

Types of Chemical Reactions are classified into several categories: synthesis (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB), and combustion (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O).
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.
pH and pOH provide logarithmic scales for expressing H⁺ and OH⁻ concentrations. pH = -log[H⁺], pOH = -log[OH⁻], and pH + pOH = 14.00 at 25°C.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Balancing Chemical Equations as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Balanced equations provide mole ratios for calculating how much product forms from given reactants, or how much reactant is needed.
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Net ionic equations show only the species that participate in the reaction, removing spectator ions.

Types of Chemical Reactions are classified into several categories: synthesis (A + B → AB), decomposition (AB → A + B), single replacement (A + BC → AC + B), double replacement (AB + CD → AD + CB), and combustion (fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Balancing Chemical Equations as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Balancing Chemical Equations as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies
Airbag deployment: 2NaN₃ → 2Na + 3N₂; precisely balanced stoichiometry ensures the correct amount of N₂ gas inflates the airbag in milliseconds

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)
Balancing combustion equations for emissions testing: ensuring complete combustion (sufficient O₂) minimizes toxic CO and particulate emissions from vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Balancing Chemical Equations applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Steel alloys: adding carbon (interstitial) to iron increases hardness; adding chromium and nickel (substitutional) creates stainless steel resistant to corrosion
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
pH meters measuring voltage differences between a glass electrode and reference electrode, converted to pH via the Nernst equation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Balancing Chemical Equations?

options.text: Airbag deployment: 2NaN₃ → 2Na + 3N₂; precisely balanced stoichiometry ensures the correct amount of N₂ gas inflates the airbag in milliseconds
Mass spectrometry separating isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio to determine elemental composition in forensics and pharmaceutical analysis
Chain reaction mechanisms in combustion: initiation produces radicals, propagation sustains the reaction, termination removes radicals (relevant to engine knocking and explosives)
Environmental chemistry: calculating the enthalpy of acid rain formation from SO₂ emissions using a series of known thermochemical equations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Balancing Chemical Equations in practice?

options.text: Cold packs using endothermic dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water to absorb heat and cool injuries
Balancing combustion equations for emissions testing: ensuring complete combustion (sufficient O₂) minimizes toxic CO and particulate emissions from vehicles
Lewis acid catalysis in organic synthesis: BF₃ and AlCl₃ as Lewis acids in Friedel-Crafts reactions for pharmaceutical manufacturing
Fluorine's extreme electronegativity making it the most reactive nonmetal, used in Teflon (PTFE) coatings and uranium enrichment (UF₆)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Balancing Chemical Equations.
